# Useful Resources {#sec-resources}

This is a curated list of resources to support your learning 
throughout this course. You do not need to master all of these 
— bookmark what is useful and return to it as needed.

## oTree

The official oTree documentation is the most reliable resource
for all questions about oTree. It is well-written, 
actively maintained, and covers fundamental concepts of what 
you will need.

Always check oTree's demo apps and common task recipes *before* 
you start writing code from scratch — sample code has likely 
already been written for what you are trying to do. The 
**Featured Projects** section on oTree Hub is a great place 
to start:

- **[Game Theory Sample Games](https://www.otreehub.com/projects/?featured=1)** 
— Classic games from economics and game theory (Prisoner's 
Dilemma, etc.). Useful for understanding how standard 
experiments are structured in oTree. Includes a 
[live demo](https://samples.demo.otreehub.com/demo) 
and [source code](https://github.com/oTree-org/otree).

- **[More Demos](https://www.otreehub.com/projects/?featured=1)** 
— A wide range of demo apps from different disciplines. A 
good starting point for finding code that is close to what 
you want to build. Includes a [live demo](https://more-demos.demo.otreehub.com/demo) and [source code](https://github.com/oTree-org/more-demos).

- **[Snippets](https://www.otreehub.com/projects/?featured=1)** 
— A collection of small recipes for common tasks in oTree. 
If you need to do something specific, check here first. 
Includes a [live demo](https://snippets.demo.otreehub.com/demo) and source code(https://github.com/oTree-org/otree-snippets).

Both the old and the new oTree forums are useful sources of 
solutions to common problems. Note that the old forum contains 
solutions written in oTree's older `self` format, so some 
details may need to be adapted to the newer `no-self` format 
used in this course. See 
[here](https://otree.readthedocs.io/en/latest/misc/noself.html) 
for more details on how the new format differs from the old one.

Finally, the **Public projects** section on oTree Hub hosts code from other oTree users that is shared publicly.

**Here is the complete list of oTree resources:**

- [Official oTree documentation](https://otree.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
- [Featured Projects on oTree Hub](https://www.otreehub.com/projects/?featured=1)
- [oTree's "Where do I put this code?" breakdown](https://www.otree.org/blog/where-to-put-code.html)
- [New oTree forum](https://www.otreehub.com/forum/)
- [Old oTree forum](https://groups.google.com/g/otree-dev)
- [Public projects](https://www.otreehub.com/projects/)

::: {.callout-tip}
## Searching for oTree help

When you run into a problem and are not sure where to start, 
try Googling your task or error message together with the word 
**"otree"** — for example, *"otree timer page"* or *"otree 
wait for all players"*. This will surface relevant results 
from the documentation, forums, and other oTree users.

If you find a result on **[Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/)**, 
read the full discussion before implementing any solution — 
do not just copy the first answer you see. The comments often 
reveal important nuance, and your use-case may require a 
slightly different approach.
:::

## Python

Python is the backbone of your oTree app — it handles the logic, stores data, and communicates between the server and the participant's browser. 
A quick tip: use `print()` to see variable values and 
outputs in the terminal while you are debugging your code.

**Web-based editors (for testing small bits of code)**

The following editors are useful when you want to quickly 
test a small piece of Python code without running it through 
oTree. This list is adapted from resources originally 
compiled by Faith Bague-Sampson.

- [Python Fiddle](http://pythonfiddle.com/)
- [myCompiler: Python](https://www.mycompiler.io/new/python)
- [PYnative Online Python Editor](https://pynative.com/online-python-code-editor-to-execute-python-code/)
